speaker: interviewer
text: It's tape number 10. We are interviewing Mr. Yehudah Bakon. Well, how was it-- how was life at the Strafcommando? 'Straf' means--

speaker: interviewee
text: Punish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Punishment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The punish colony.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was an area of punishment next to where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was in this same compound, but we came there just to be isolated from the rest. There were, as I told you, three blocks isolated in the main D camp, which was the Mannerlager, the men's camp, where most of the people and their jobs were going from this camp. But in this D, or Mannerlager, where the head of the Mannerlager was a very bad criminal man, French-Danish. And we knew him very well. These three blocks where the Strafcommando, which was block number 13 and block number 11 and nine, which were the Sonderkommando-- they were closed. And nobody could go in. I mean, nobody would even like to go in because it meant to be put to punishment.

speaker: interviewee
text: These men were like lepers. Nobody could--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- nobody should-- there shouldn't be any contact with them. But we children came there because they wanted, somehow, we shouldn't mix with the other people for one reason or another. We don't know till today what was their intention-- to keep us for the Red Cross, or to send us for exchange. We don't know. But anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was after the selection? After you were only 14?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the whole time, I mean, from July-- from the middle of July, when we came there, till the 18 of January '45 when we left Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mean all the time there was selections in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: From time to time there were selections. But even the Sonderkommando who was living in these blocks and had to go daily to work in the crematorium-- later on, they had to live in the crematorium. Mainly, that was the crematorium number one and number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: To live there? They stayed. They didn't go back to the block.

words.text: They stayed. They didn't go back to the block.

speaker: interviewee
text: To live there. On the roof, there were some special railings for them built.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't-- well, they're very afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were so busy?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were busy and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the time when the Hungarian Jews arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- we were witnessing the whole annihilation of the Hungarian Jews, I told you, from April, when we were still in the Familienlager. And later on, we could see all the transports till the late autumn from Hungaria. But me-- from time to time, came other transports from Paris, I remember-- I remember once came to our block, to the Strafcommando, straight from Champs-Elysees. A young father and a young boy about 11 or 12 came to us. And we didn't know why and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews. And we couldn't speak French. But it was very strange. From time to time, they came-- single people, for one reason or another. Also, some other German, [NON-ENGLISH] they took from the gypsy camp. But later on, block number nine and also 11-- when block 11 was a kind of little [INAUDIBLE] first aid, a laboratorium-- I mean, first aid when somebody broke or got something. So they had their own doctor. I remember he was from a Czech origin. And even I, myself, when I got something on my knee, and they had they best medication, of course, which they took from the parcels from the people who had been gassed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had the best things. And they gave-- he gave me there, on this block number 11, the first aid. But these two blocks were also emptied, selection and so on, all the rest of the people went to live in the crematorium. And slowly, also the straf company was emptied. In the end-- But I want to ask you about the straf.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they came, all kinds of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see, actually, people being punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were next to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In our courtyard there was this famous--

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to your block there was a courtyard.

speaker: interviewee
text: In our courtyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It belonged to our block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there were the gallows, portable gallows, and a kind of table for punishment. And sometimes we witnessed when people, let's say, some people got the punishment for two or three months to be in the Strafcommando and 40 lashes and so on. And this 40 lashes, they-- somebody had to count. Sometimes the prisoner had to count it and the SS man or sometimes two SS men, but they were so tired from this job. They were lashing them. And sometimes we were allowed to see it. Sometimes we had to be in the block, and we could look through the wood and see what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there also torture there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember one prisoner tried to escape. And he was caught. And he was terribly tortured. And I think I told you about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the straf.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Strafcommando by SS. And the huge stick, which we were carrying the barrels of soap, he broke on him. And he was running. And it was unbearable to look. It was, even for us, who where hardened a bit-- terribly. And I also remember once they caught three people. One of them was a Russian by the name [NON-ENGLISH]. And he tried to commit suicide. He cut his veins. And that was, let's say, in the early morning. And he had to be hanged in the afternoon when all the people came back from the work, which would be around 4 or 6 o'clock. And this SS man said, this man had to live till the hanging. And they brought the best equipment. They sent a car and phone, I don't know what else, to bring a doctor and bandage--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he cut his wrists?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to keep him alive. And I remember they schlepped him completely. He was white and hardly could walk. But they still hanged him. And this hanging, all of us had to march-- it was before the kitchen. There was a big-- in the beginning of the Mannerlager-- and near the kitchens, they put up some gallows, just the string. And these three people were hanged. And the orchestra had to play. And that was his execution. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there were also Russian--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Germans behave towards you? These same Germans, the Nazis who were in the Strafcommando, how did they behave towards you, children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they came-- to us, well, again, unpredictable. Let's say there was one SS man, Stefan Baretzki. He was famous for his brutality. And I saw many times how he finished some people-- some prisoners when he, for one reason or another, he didn't like them. So he could beat them up that they couldn't get up anymore. And most probably they were killed. But on the other hand, these same SS men came to our block and even put up a ping pong table and played with us. And the same Baretzki, once, in the evening, he just choose 10 people from the whole compound of the Mannerlager and said, come to the SS [NON-ENGLISH], which means to the gate, which is most dangerous. We  knew what it meant, to be tortured, or killed, or something like that. And he came out. We were staying in a row, 10 different people. I was one of them. And he took a huge salami, cut it in 10 pieces, gave to each one, one piece, and said, go. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the taste of the salami?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember more how afraid I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the same Baretzki-- and also our block had [NON-ENGLISH] Bednarek-- much later, I think around '64, I witnessed--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean? Oh, [INAUDIBLE]. '64.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Frankfurt was the Auschwitz trial. And I was there to witness. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was there? He was alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: --met again, Baretzki and Bednarek. And I had to say what I know. And by the way, I remember that all SS people denied that they have done this or that till the very end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw, you remembered him from then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, yeah. Of course I could recognize them. And only Baretzki even repented. He took-- he's the only one. He was, at that time, a very young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way did he repent? What did he say?

words.text: In what way did he repent? What did

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very sorry. And he said, yes, I have done it. And he was sorry. That's interesting. From all other German--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you say that he was a terrible criminal.

words.text: But you say that he was

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, he was a terrible criminal. But I witnessed that even the worst SS men had, [INTERPOSING VOICES] here and there-- I mean, in the camp-- a kind of spark of humanity. And maybe because we children were still looking a bit more--

speaker: interviewer
text: Human.

speaker: interviewee
text: --human than the other one, or maybe they-- we reminded them of somebody. So they behaved differently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did meet the famous Orchestra of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We knew them. And we talked with them. And I even remember the songs or the music that [? to ?] play. They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they play, actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually Sundays, but always-- or nearly always when all the work was-- I mean, all the prisoners came back from work, or sometimes even when they went to work. But Sundays, for the pleasure of the SS. And I remember the song, [NON-ENGLISH] and other very kitschy Viennese and other German songs, plays. And I remember later on, they were disbanded, and they had also to work in the-- or in a kind of Rollwagen [? commander. ?] But they were older. I mean, we were children, and they were, let's say, in the late 20s, 30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all musicians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, former musicians. And now they had to do the same work like us. I remember once, I met them on the way when we went to the crematorium for wood and so on. And they were very badly treated. I mean, they were really beaten up by the Germans and was no opportunity that-- if--

speaker: interviewer
text: They survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- maybe some of them survived. I remember another woman who wasn't in this orchestra in Birkenau-- because I was in the Mannerlager. But she had polio and was limping. And she told me when she came to Auschwitz, Mengele saw her, that she was limping. And I ask her what he said. And she told him she had an operation. And he was interested to know who was the doctor who performed the operation. And he left her-- because of his so-called medical interest-- into the camp. And her job was transcription of the notes for the orchestra in Auschwitz. And she told me, when she came to Israel, she had here some relatives who came just before the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: --relatives-- before the war. And when she came, her relatives said, oh, how strange that you survived. Only whores could survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: So that wasn't so pleasant. Yeah. Only somebody who sold himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Well, now, the front is coming near.

speaker: interviewee
text: And slowly the camp is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Evacuating.

speaker: interviewee
text: --evacuating. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: But not in the sense of really killing people.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're just taking them out on these marches.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were taken-- taken out, because I remember they [? weren't ?] gassing. In the late autumn, it stopped. In the very late autumn, there was not any more gassings. They only burned in the crematorium some Musulmans they brought. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: About the Musulmans I want to speak to you, because you-- you said you tried-- you saw the Musulmans, and you kept yourself from becoming a Musulman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the reason was very simple.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did several things in order to prevent that. What's a Musulman, actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew-- Musulman was an expression, a name in Auschwitz, it's nothing to do with Mohammed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Muslim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody who lost the will to live and who is completely-- he doesn't care anymore what's happening around. And of course, he loses, slowly, weight and doesn't care how he looks. And in Auschwitz, it was most important to keep, somehow--

speaker: interviewer
text: An appearance

speaker: interviewee
text: --clean, and because this was the very first one who were beaten up or sent to the crematorium, et cetera. And these Musulmans, you could see, oh, he-- he's on the way to the crematorium-- or, as we called it in our children language, with one leg in the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So you were aware of that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we tried--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and tried to prevent this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to look like it--

speaker: interviewer
text: And not to be  one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not to be one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried, thanks to the education we got from Theresienstadt and mainly from Fredy Hirsch. He was very punctually on it that we should be always clean and correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you personally-- you personally, you were not a very strong boy, and you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how-- what we have done all together in Auschwitz-- everyone tried to get out of heavy, physical work, which you couldn't endure. And we were lucky that our work wasn't killing. But even this work we had to do, everyone tried to work as less as possible. And be aware. Let's say we took breaks. But in the minute we saw some SS, we said, achtzehn. 'Achtzehn' means 18, but it sounds like 'achtung,' beware.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we suddenly shouted, achtzehn, which sounds like achtung. And achtung means beware. And then at once, of course, we pretended that we worked with all our might. And it depended who was your kapo or who was supervising your work. If he was good natured, so he, himself said, oh, pretend or show off that you work with the last-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that you do something. You are working hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the danger was you are caught that you don't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you have to be very clever to pretend. And if you can, not to work at all. And my philosophy--

speaker: interviewer
text: What you did was to prevent the fact of losing strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: Losing strength.

speaker: interviewer
text: To keep your strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I know, also, there was a time when we could choose to go on a transport, meaning that we have a choice-- as if a choice-- to go out of Auschwitz-- I mean, from the danger to be gassed. But the danger-- our danger was that we will be-- we will be dying in the work--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hard work.

speaker: interviewee
text: --out work and hard work, or by-- be killed off by the SS men and so on. I, because I was so weak and small, choose-- I said, as long as I am here, well, maybe I will end in the gas chambers, but this is only suffering for a comparatively short time. But as long as I'm here, I know how to get enough food not to starve. Whereupon to go out, I didn't know what will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were also intelligent, and you had a good instinct.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most probably. Well, what I was, at that time, is very curious. It was just in puberty. And I wanted to know what's going on. And something was pushing me-- I have to remember. And I was drawing, also, in the camp. I had some papers from time to time with a pencil. But of course, these drawings I had to hide or they were lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have them, still?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I reconstructed them at once after the war-- at once when I was able to walk and to draw. After I got my typhoid. And I think it was in July '45, I made these drawings about the crematorium, et cetera, and the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe you will show some--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I will show you afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we should go back to-- to the march. Finally-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Before the march, I would like to mention something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even the block 13 was liquidated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Evacuated. And we came to a different block, number 29, which was a children block, a Jugend block. What happened-- there came so many transports from Hungaria, from Greece, some from Yugoslavia, a few partisans, and mainly from Slovakia. They-- and the crematorium, at that time, was so busy with gassing all the Hungarian Jews that the overflow came to the camp, and mainly to our camp. And here, suddenly, there was a new block of youngsters between, I would guess, 14 and 16, 17. The kapo of this block, on block number 29, was Hans [? Erlinger. ?] I even remember his numbers-- [NON-ENGLISH], 71 302. He was a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was a German or, I think, a political prisoner, but very kind and good to us children. He was a sick man. Later on, he accompanied us, even, to Mauthausen. And he was extremely--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Erlinger? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Hans Erlinger. ?] And he was very kind to all of the children. And he took us with the Rollwagen. And so and always somebody else had to accompany us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what condition were all the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what condition were the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it depends from where they come. Some-- some of the children came straight from Slovakia, so they were in better condition. I remember some of these children once came to us, and I asked them, from where are you, and what happened to you? And they said, well, we were just in the gas chambers. We were naked. And then came Schwarzhuber. And the half of the children who were just entering the gas chambers-- said, out. He doesn't know why, but he was-- this group of children were in the gas chambers. The half went in. And the half, Schwarzhuber in the last moment, took out. By the way, there was always a competition, a kind, who is a better killer, Schwarzhuber or Mengele. All of them made selection, Dr. Thilo and Dr. Klein. But in our case, maybe I told you that we went twice through selection. First, Schwarzhuber selected us. We didn't-- and a few days later, Mengele was-- Schwarzhuber took his job. So he made another selection of the same group. But these Hungarian or Slovakian children, I don't know today, exactly--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were sort of competing on making these selections. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, depends, everyone had his-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody who made selection was an important man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't think so. But Schwarzhuber was the head of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the head of the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the whole Birkenau camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Head of Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And Mengele was the head-- we called him, of course, the Death Angel. And we saw him several times. But he was the head of the medical and experiments, et cetera. And these children also, later, got a similar job as ours. But slowly, they were sent off--

speaker: interviewer
text: To Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Germany. We don't know where, but to different camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not to gas.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to gas because later on, there was no more gassing. Maybe some of them did or-- but I remember only this group of children who told me this story. And later on, just before the 18 of January, we knew something would be happen because there was a great nervous-ity in the camp. I even remember Christmas '44. We had to take, with our Rollwagen, a Christmas tree to some of the SS compound, from one camp to another. That was also one of our jobs. But usually, the job was--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were having fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans still had some fun, but they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were nervous.

speaker: interviewee
text: --nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And you were preparing now? Did you know that you will soon leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that something is happening. We could-- in the last days, we could even hear the [? cannon, ?] the shelling from far away. And our friend who had better position tried to provide us with better clothes, and this I told you, with shoes, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So that you will be prepared for the march. And when--

words.text: Yes. So that you will be

speaker: interviewee
text: When it happened--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out that you are leaving? Who told you, today we go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were-- we saw around us. One camp after the other was emptied. We went still with the Rollwagen, I remember, to the abandoned [NON-ENGLISH], which was next to us. And there was nobody there anymore. And why do I remember it? Because we went in the abandoned kitchen. Now, only the Mannerlager were still few-- I mean, some maybe, a couple of thousand prisoners. And we saw salt in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Salt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Salt. And we took it in the camp, and we made business with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, salt was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Something very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something very important, yes. You didn't have salt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in such-- but suddenly, we saw a lot of salt. And we brought it, and we sold it to other prisoners. And for that we got, let's say, four cigarettes. And four cigarettes was equivalent of a ration or a half of bread, or whatever we got. I remember the cigarettes, by the way, a little before, were [? Yosma ?] and Corfu because the Greek people who, at that time in summer '44, the Germans, I think, occupied Corfu or one of these islands, and these prisoners brought with them [? Yosma ?] and Corfu. I remember, even, a group of Greek Jews who came to our camp. And it was difficult to speak with them because they spoke only Greek. But we could somehow, in between us, understand each other. By the way, once, to Theresienstadt, came a group of children from Bialystok--

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which were isolated. And they had to live in Theresienstadt completely isolated. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because they came from Bialystok. And people who had some contact with them told us they were terribly afraid to take a shower. And they were starting to scream, 'Gas! Gas!' And we only know that there were some volunteers who worked with them from Theresienstadt--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That had to be '43, autumn '43, before we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these  children knew exactly--

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew, but therefore, they were isolated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that they wouldn't [? speak. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: But Fredy Hirsch somehow was caught with the children, and they said his punishment was that he had to go afterwards, in September, to Auschwitz. But these children--

speaker: interviewer
text: He made contact with these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Illegal. But there were some workers and doctors who volunteered to work with these children. And later on, they were sent to the east. And we know from the Sonderkommando that all these children and all the volunteers and doctors who--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who worked with them-- --were with the group went to the gas chambers. How many children were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Must be around 100, 200, to even more. But this group--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason they brought them to Theresienstadt? You don't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, we don't know. Most probably, they-- when they annihilated the ghetto, they had in mind to do something with the children, maybe for exchange. And as I told you, the Red Cross, at that time, was interested in the ghetto, Theresienstadt. And they were afraid the German Red Cross, they will need a cover, also for the people who went to the east. And therefore, most probably, we were kept, the Czech family camp, and maybe also our group of children. But that's unknown.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We should stop now. And soon go on.
